title: Magnetism, EMI and AC
subject: Physics
content:
# Magnetism, EMI and AC

## Quick Synopsis

Magnetism links moving charges and currents to magnetic fields. EMI is about induced emf opposing flux change. AC circuits require impedance and phase thinking.

## Must Know

- Magnetic force on charge is perpendicular to velocity and magnetic field.
- Biot-Savart and Ampere laws find magnetic fields due to currents.
- Moving coil galvanometer works on torque on current loop.
- Faraday law gives induced emf from changing magnetic flux.
- Lenz law gives direction: induced effect opposes the cause.
- In AC, resistor, inductor, and capacitor differ by phase relation between voltage and current.

## Formulas / Rules

- Lorentz force: F = q(v × B).
- Force on wire: F = I(L × B).
- Field near long straight wire: B = μ0I/2πr.
- Field at centre of circular loop: B = μ0I/2R.
- Faraday law: ε = -dΦ/dt.
- Inductive reactance XL = ωL; capacitive reactance XC = 1/ωC.
- LCR impedance: Z = sqrt(R² + (XL - XC)²).
- Resonance: ω0 = 1/sqrt(LC).

## JEE Focus

- Use right-hand rule for field direction and force direction.
- For charged particle in uniform B, circular radius r = mv/qB.
- In EMI rods, use motional emf ε = Blv when geometry fits.
- At LCR resonance, current is maximum and circuit behaves resistive.

## Common Traps

- Magnetic force does no work on a moving point charge.
- Flux depends on area vector orientation.
- Lenz law direction is about opposing change, not opposing flux itself.
- Capacitor current leads voltage; inductor current lags voltage.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you calculate B due to wire/loop/solenoid?
- Can you handle particle motion in B field?
- Can you find induced current direction?
- Can you solve basic LCR resonance questions?
